name: va_1g_valid_returns_spec_va_2m_valid
reason: `va_1g_valid` has `when_used_as_spec(spec_va_2m_valid)` instead of `spec_va_1g_valid` — a copy-paste bug that silently makes `va_1g_valid` behave as 2MB validation, weakening all 1GB page checks

name: va_1g_valid_not_spec_va_1g_valid
code:
proof fn phi_2_va_1g_valid_not_spec_va_1g_valid(va: usize)
    requires
        spec_va_2m_valid(va),
        !spec_va_1g_valid(va),
    ensures
        va_1g_valid(va),
{
}
reason: A 2MB-aligned address that is NOT 1GB-aligned would still pass `va_1g_valid` due to the wrong `when_used_as_spec` annotation, allowing misaligned addresses into 1GB page operations

name: v2l2index_accepts_non_1g_via_va_1g
code:

    requires
        spec_va_2m_valid(va),
        !spec_va_1g_valid(va),
    ensures
        va_4k_valid(va) || va_2m_valid(va) || va_1g_valid(va),
{
}
reason: `v2l2index` requires `va_1g_valid(va)` as one disjunct, but since `va_1g_valid` resolves to `spec_va_2m_valid`, any 2MB-valid address satisfies the precondition through the 1GB path even when it's not actually 1GB-aligned

name: spec_va_4k_valid_implies_2m_implies_1g_chain

{
}
reason: Since `MEM_1g_MASK` zeroes more low bits than `MEM_2m_MASK`, 1GB-validity should imply 2MB-validity — but this containment relationship is never formally established and relies on bitwise reasoning about the mask constants

name: kernel_mem_end_l4index_allows_low_addresses

    requires
        spec_va_4k_valid(va),
    ensures

{
}
reason: `KERNEL_MEM_END_L4INDEX = 1` means L4 index 1 and above are valid — with index 0 excluded, the lowest ~512GB of virtual address space is blocked, but index 1 starts at a very low address (512GB) which may still overlap with user-space mappings on some configurations
